# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  WEEK 1  |  DAY 1  |  VARIABLES AND DATA TYPES
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#
#  LEARNING OBJECTIVES
#  ───────────────────
#  1. Create and name variables using Python snake_case convention
#  2. Identify the 4 core data types: str, int, float, bool
#  3. Use the type\(\) function to inspect and verify data types
#
#  TIME:  ~30 minutes  \(3 concepts × 10 min each\)
#
#  YOUTUBE
#  ───────
#  Search: "Python variables and data types for beginners"
#  Search: "Python type\(\) function explained"
#
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════


# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  CONCEPT 1 — VARIABLES
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#
#  A variable is a named container that stores a value in memory.
#  You create one with a single = sign:  name = value
#
#  Naming rules \(snake_case convention\):
#    - All lowercase letters
#    - Words separated by underscores
#    - Must start with a letter or underscore, NOT a number
#    - No spaces or special characters \(except underscore\)
#
#  Good names:  employee_name, total_sales, is_active, record_count
#  Bad names:   Employee Name, 1stValue, total-sales, TotalSales
#
#  You can reassign a variable at any time — the new value replaces the old one.
#  Python figures out the type automatically; you do not declare it yourself.
#
# EXAMPLE ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────

employee_name   = "Sarah Levi"   # text value — wrap in quotes
employee_age    = 34             # whole number — no quotes
employee_salary = 95000.0        # decimal number — no quotes
is_active       = True           # True/False — capital first letter, no quotes

print\(employee_name\)
print\(employee_age\)
print\(employee_salary\)
print\(is_active\)

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  EXERCISE 1
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#
#  Create 4 variables with these exact names and values:
#    customer_name  = "David Cohen"
#    customer_age   = 45
#    credit_score   = 720.5
#    is_premium     = True
#
#  Then print each variable on its own line \(4 print statements\).
#
#  Expected output:
#      David Cohen
#      45
#      720.5
#      True
#




ENDOFFILE)

import sys, io

W1   = BASE + '/Week_1_Python_Basics'
W2   = BASE + '/Week_2_Functions'

HR  = '\\u2500' * 46

def write_file\(path, content\):
    with open\(path, 'w', encoding='utf-8'\) as f:
        f.write\(content\)
    print\('Written:', path\)

'#  CONCEPT 1 -- VARIABLES\\n'

'#\\n'
'#  Naming rules \(snake_case convention\):\\n'
'#    - All lowercase letters\\n'
'#    - Words separated by underscores\\n'
'#    - Must start with a letter or underscore, NOT a number\\n'
'#    - No spaces or special characters \(except underscore\)\\n'
'#\\n'
'#  Good names:  employee_name, total_sales, is_active, record_count\\n'
'#  Bad names:   Employee Name, 1stValue, total-sales, TotalSales\\n'
'#\\n'
'#  You can reassign a variable at any time -- the new value replaces the old one.\\n'
'#  Python figures out the type automatically; you do not declare it yourself.\\n'
'#\\n'
f'# EXAMPLE {HR[:54]}\\n'
'\\n'
'employee_name   = "Sarah Levi"   # text value -- wrap in quotes\\n'
'employee_age    = 34             # whole number -- no quotes\\n'
'employee_salary = 95000.0        # decimal number -- no quotes\\n'
'is_active       = True           # True/False -- capital first letter, no quotes\\n'
'\\n'
'print\(employee_name\)\\n'
'print\(employee_age\)\\n'
'print\(employee_salary\)\\n'
'print\(is_active\)\\n'
'\\n'
f'# {EQ}\\n'
'#  EXERCISE 1\\n'
f'# {EQ}\\n'
'#\\n'
'#  Create 4 variables with these exact names and values:\\n'
'#    customer_name  = "David Cohen"\\n'
'#    customer_age   = 45\\n'
'#    credit_score   = 720.5\\n'
'#    is_premium     = True\\n'
'#\\n'
'#  Then print each variable on its own line \(4 print statements\).\\n'
'#\\n'
'#  Expected output:\\n'
'#      David Cohen\\n'
'#      45\\n'
'#      720.5\\n'
'#      True\\n'
'#\\n'
'\\n'
'\\n'
'\\n'
'\\n'
'\\n'
f'# {EQ}\\n'
'#  CONCEPT 2 -- THE 4 CORE DATA TYPES\\n'
f'# {EQ}\\n'
'#\\n'
'#  Python has 4 basic data types you will use constantly in data work:\\n'
'#\\n'
'#    str   -- text, enclosed in quotes:  "hello",  "123",  "True"\\n'
'#    int   -- whole number, no decimal:  42,  -7,  0\\n'
'#    float -- decimal number:  3.14,  100.0,  -0.5\\n'
'#    bool  -- exactly two values:  True  or  False  \(capital first letter, no quotes\)\\n'
'#\\n'
'#  COMMON MISTAKE 1: putting a number inside quotes makes it a string, not a number.\\n'
'#    price = "29.99"  -- this is str -- you CANNOT do math with it\\n'
'#    price =  29.99   -- this is float -- you CAN do math with it\\n'
'#\\n'
'#  COMMON MISTAKE 2: forgetting quotes around text values.\\n'
'#    city = Tel Aviv   -- Python crashes \(it looks for a variable named Tel\)\\n'
'#    city = "Tel Aviv" -- correct\\n'
'#\\n'
f'# EXAMPLE {HR[:54]}\\n'
'\\n'
'product_name   = "Wireless Mouse"   # str   -- text in quotes\\n'
'product_price  = 49.99              # float -- decimal, no quotes\\n'
'units_in_stock = 200                # int   -- whole number, no quotes\\n'
'is_available   = True               # bool  -- capital T, no quotes\\n'
'\\n'
'print\(product_name\)\\n'
'print\(product_price\)\\n'
'print\(units_in_stock\)\\n'
'print\(is_available\)\\n'
'\\n'
f'# {EQ}\\n'
'#  EXERCISE 2\\n'
f'# {EQ}\\n'
'#\\n'
'#  The 4 lines below contain 2 type mistakes.\\n'
'#  Copy all 4 lines into your exercise area, fix the 2 wrong ones,\\n'
'#  then print all 4 variables.\\n'
'#\\n'
'#  Lines to copy and fix:\\n'
'#    order_id      = "10042"    <- WRONG type -- should be an integer\\n'
'#    order_total   = 250.75     <- correct\\n'
'#    customer_city = Tel Aviv   <- WRONG -- will crash, add the missing quotes\\n'
'#    is_shipped    = True       <- correct\\n'
'#\\n'
'#  Expected output:\\n'
'#      10042\\n'
'#      250.75\\n'
'#      Tel Aviv\\n'
'#      True\\n'
'#\\n'
'\\n'
'\\n'
'\\n'
'\\n'
'\\n'
f'# {EQ}\\n'
'#  CONCEPT 3 -- THE type\(\) FUNCTION\\n'
f'# {EQ}\\n'
'#\\n'
'#  type\(value\) returns the data type of any value or variable.\\n'
'#  Output looks like:  <class str>  or  <class int>  etc.\\n'
'#\\n'
'#  When to use it:\\n'
'#    - When you receive data from a file or API and need to confirm types\\n'
'#    - When a number looks correct but behaves unexpectedly -- type\(\) reveals the truth\\n'
'#    - When debugging: "why can not I do math on this number?"\\n'
'#\\n'
'#  You can combine type\(\) with print\(\) to show value and type on one line:\\n'
'#    print\(value, "->", type\(value\)\)\\n'
'#\\n'
'#  This pattern is extremely common in data cleaning and debugging.\\n'
'#\\n'
f'# EXAMPLE {HR[:54]}\\n'
'\\n'
'# Print just the type of each employee variable\\n'
'print\(type\(employee_name\)\)      # <class str>\\n'
'print\(type\(employee_age\)\)       # <class int>\\n'
'print\(type\(employee_salary\)\)    # <class float>\\n'
'print\(type\(is_active\)\)          # <class bool>\\n'
'\\n'
'# Print value and type together on one line\\n'
'print\(employee_name,    "->", type\(employee_name\)\)\\n'
'print\(employee_age,     "->", type\(employee_age\)\)\\n'
'print\(employee_salary,  "->", type\(employee_salary\)\)\\n'
'print\(is_active,        "->", type\(is_active\)\)\\n'
'\\n'
f'# {EQ}\\n'
'#  EXERCISE 3\\n'
f'# {EQ}\\n'
'#\\n'
'#  Five variables are defined for you below as starting data -- do not change them.\\n'

'#  Use the same pattern shown in the example above.\\n'
'#\\n'
'#  Expected output:\\n'
"#      2024 -> <class 'str'>\\n"
"#      2024 -> <class 'int'>\\n"
"#      20.24 -> <class 'float'>\\n"
"#      True -> <class 'str'>\\n"
"#      True -> <class 'bool'>\\n"
'#\\n'
'\\n'
'# --- starting data \(do not change these lines\) ---\\n'
'field_a = "2024"\\n'
'field_b = 2024\\n'
'field_c = 20.24\\n'
'field_d = "True"\\n'
'field_e = True\\n'
'\\n'
'\\n'
'\\n'
'\\n'
\)
